coredata.dc:description: The blood glucose concentration in aquatic organisms, a crucial indicator reflecting their health status, holds significant importance for detecting glucose levels in serum in terms of processing and quality monitoring. In this study, a novel POD biomimetic enzyme (p-BEs) with horseradish peroxidase catalytic properties was designed, optimized, and its mechanism was discussed in detail. Based on this, a portable system has been developed capable of determining glucose levels in three ways: quantitatively analyzed through UV–Vis/MD, quantitatively analyzed on-site using a mobile phone RGB, and semi-quantitatively analyzed through a drip plate. Meanwhile, compared with other catalytic methods for detecting glucose, we achieved a lower limit of detection (0.03 μM) and shorter detection time (12 min), with high catalytic activity. This study provides new insights into the design of efficient and reliable cascade catalytic systems responsive to glucose, offering a low-cost, simplicity of operation method for glucose detection.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The blood glucose concentration in aquatic organisms, a crucial indicator reflecting their health status, holds significant importance for detecting glucose levels in serum in terms of processing and quality monitoring. In this study, a novel POD biomimetic enzyme (p-BEs) with horseradish peroxidase catalytic properties was designed, optimized, and its mechanism was discussed in detail. Based on this, a portable system has been developed capable of determining glucose levels in three ways: quantitatively analyzed through UV–Vis/MD, quantitatively analyzed on-site using a mobile phone RGB, and semi-quantitatively analyzed through a drip plate. Meanwhile, compared with other catalytic methods for detecting glucose, we achieved a lower limit of detection (0.03 μM) and shorter detection time (12 min), with high catalytic activity. This study provides new insights into the design of efficient and reliable cascade catalytic systems responsive to glucose, offering a low-cost, simplicity of operation method for glucose detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Fish represent a crucial source of high-quality protein for human consumption [1]. Distinctive species such as Oncorhynchus mykiss, Siniperca chuatsi, and Hucho taimen are vital resources in the northeast region. In vertebrates, including most fish species, glucose serves as a significant source of energy and carbon, making blood glucose concentration a key indicator of overall fish health [2]. Unlike mammals, fish utilize glucose generated through gluconeogenesis and glycogen breakdown processes, maintaining specific blood glucose levels through circulatory mechanisms and hormones like cortisol and insulin. Stress responses, triggered by external stressors coordinated by the hypothalamus-pituitary-interrenal axis, directly reflect the survival status of fish [3]. Factors such as overcrowding, oxygen deficiency, poor water quality due to residual food and feces accumulation, or suboptimal rearing and transportation conditions can elevate fish blood glucose levels. Monitoring changes in fish blood glucose levels provides valuable indicators of their physiological activities [4].

Fish blood glucose levels typically range between 1 and 10 mmol/L, varying among different species. Various methods, including chromatometry [5], fluorescent spectrometry [6], liquid chromatography [7], electrochemical process [8], spectrophotometry [9], and LC-MS [10], have been employed for glucose detection. However, these methods for glucose detection often suffer from limitations such as complex sample processing, lengthy analysis times, expensive instrumentation, and the need for specialized expertise [11]. Optical detection methods, including spectrophotometry and chromatometry, have gained interest due to their simplicity, good reproducibility, and cost-effectiveness. Nevertheless, traditional optical methods with a single output signal are highly susceptible to environmental fluctuations, resulting in reduced accuracy. Therefore, there is a need to develop a new, highly sensitive sensing platform for spectrophotometry and chromatometry for glucose detection [12]. Currently, there is a lack of dedicated methods for determining glucose levels in fish serum. Conventional methods, while effective, suffer from prolonged sample processing times, leading to changes in glucose concentrations before obtaining results, making real-time monitoring impractical. High-end instruments like LC-MS, while sensitive and with low detection limits, require significant financial and time investments, along with specialized personnel, making them unsuitable for on-the-ground use by local fishermen. Hence, the development of an efficient, simple, and convenient glucose detection method is essential.

Recently, biomimetic enzymes have garnered significant interest as a new class of materials due to their simplicity in manufacturing and excellent biocompatibility [13-15,52]. In addition to these features, some biomimetic enzymes exhibit high enzyme-like activity, overcoming the drawbacks of traditional artificial enzyme mimics, such as high manufacturing and maintenance costs and unstable properties [16–18]. Different structured biomimetic enzymes show varied enzyme-like activities, such as oxidase-like and peroxidase-like activities [19–21]. The detection strategies can be applied to various life substances, including cholesterol [22], homocysteine [23], uric acid [24], and ascorbic acid [25]. Glucose oxidase (GOD) is a widely used method in clinical glucose detection due to its simplicity, specificity, and high accuracy. GOD catalyzes the oxidation of glucose to gluconic acid and H2O2. H2O2 under the catalysis of horseradish peroxidase (HRP), oxidizes a colorless substrate, producing a colored product. The amount of the product was proportional to the glucose concentration, and the concentration could be calculated by measuring the absorbance of the solution. Biomimetic enzymes, with their numerous advantages, had found wide application in glucose detection. Utilizing biomimetic enzymes with peroxidase-like activity in place of HRP in conjunction with GOD catalyzes the glucose-induced H2O2 production. The peroxidase-like biomimetic enzyme then catalyzes the oxidation of the substrate, leading to a color change, allowing determination of glucose levels in biological fluids [26]. Commercial assay kits utilize this principle for glucose detection, however HRP in these kits is highly influenced by temperature and pH, and the kits are costly. Our novel biomimetical horseradish peroxidase could catalyze hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to produce ·OH, ·OH could oxidize colorless substrates to colored substances [27]. Therefore, biomimetic enzymes with enzyme-like activity, as nanosensors had enormous potential for detecting H2O2 and its related molecules using chromatometry and spectrophotometry.

In this study, we utilized glutamic acid and Co as raw materials to design and optimize a novel biomimetic enzyme with enzymatic catalytic properties, named POD biomimetic enzyme (p-BEs). Based on this, we developed a portable glucose rapid detection system based on cascade catalysis. The system employs p-BEs as catalysts. Under the catalytic action, glucose undergoes cascade catalysis, ultimately oxidizing the substrate to a visually observable blue color. Accurate qualitative and quantitative analysis of glucose content was performed using UV–Vis or MD instruments. Interestingly, visual detection methods were designed using color changes, with smartphones serving as detectors through a specially designed program. Smartphones acted as RGB extractors to identify glucose levels, enabling rapid and portable visual detection of glucose. Homemade color cards and drip plates were also utilized for quick and efficient qualitative and semi-quantitative analysis of glucose. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of these detection methods in accurately measuring glucose levels in real fish samples. This study provides new insights into the design of efficient and reliable detection systems based on glucose-responsive cascade catalysis and offers a novel method for glucose detection in fish.

The preparation of p-BEs is illustrated in Fig. 1A In a 250 mL beaker, L-glutamic acid (food grade) 0.9 g and CoCl2·6H2O 0.9 g were accurately weighed and dissolved in 90 mL ultrapure water. The mixed solution was transferred to a 100 mL PTFE-lined container, placed in a stainless steel reaction vessel, and reacted at 200 °C for 10 h. After the reaction, the mixture in the reactor was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min to obtain the supernatant. The supernatant was purified and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Subsequently, it was concentrated using rotary evaporation to obtain a highly concentrated pink solution. After cooling to room temperature, it was placed in a dialysis bag and dialyzed for 48 h in ultrapure water. The filtered aqueous solution was frozen at −80 °C, further freeze-dried at −60 °C, yielding a blue solid powder. The powder was ground, placed in a centrifuge tube, and reconstituted with ultrapure water for use. n-BEs were prepared similarly, excluding the addition of CoCl2·6H2O.

L-Glutamic acid and CoCl2·6H2O were provided by Kermel (Tianjin, China). Fisher (Pittsburgh, PA, USA) supplied MnCl2·4H2O, Mg (NO3)2·6H2O, KCl, CaCl2, ZnSO4·7H2O, NaCl, and other reagents. H2O2 and glucose were provided by Beida Jubang Science and Technology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). GOD, POD, ABTS, TMB, OPD, DMPO, PH, HAc-NaAc, IPA, HIS, SOD, and PBS were supplied by Anpel Company (ShangHai, China).

TEM (JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan) was used to characterize the morphology and structure of p-BEs. FTIR (Nicolet, Madison, USA) analyzed the composition and surface functional groups of materials in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. XRD (Panalytical, Empyrean, Netherlands) measured the diffraction angle from 10 to 90° and recorded crystal structure-related parameters. XPS (PE, Minnesota, USA) utilized ThermoScientific K-Alpha+ with a monochromatic AlKα X-ray source to determine total and fine spectra. ESR spectra were obtained using a micro-ESR (Bruker EMX plus-6/1, Germany). UV-VIS absorption spectra were recorded on U4100UV-VIS (Hitachi Limited, Japan) and MD (Molecular Device, USA). RGB values were recorded on Xiaomi 10 smartphones.

Under 37 °C conditions, steady-state kinetic studies were conducted. Generally, peroxidase activity is studied through Michaelis-Menten enzyme kinetics equation. We added buffer solution (HAc-NaAc 340 μL, pH = 4, 0.2 M), p-BEs (20 μL), TMB, and H2O2, with TMB and H2O2 as substrates for catalytic reactions. The detection of the variation in absorbance at 652 nm in the catalytic system was achieved by varying the substrate concentrations. The initial reaction rate was computed in accordance with Lambert-Beer's law. The catalytic reaction kinetics parameters (Km, Vmax) of p-BEs were obtained by fitting the Michaelis-Menten dynamic equation V =

Where v is the initial rate of the catalytic reaction, Km represents the Michaelis constant, Vmax represents the maximum reaction rate and [S] is the substrate concentration.

DMPO was used as an electron capture agent to verify ·OH generation by ESR spectroscopy. The detection schemes for three groups of samples were set as follows: Group A comprised HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4, 0.2 M) + p-BEs (20 μL) + DMPO (20 μL, 0.1 M) + H2O2 (20 μL, 100 mM); Group B comprised DMPO (20 μL, 0.1 M) + H2O2 (20 μL, 100 mM); Group C comprised p-BEs (20 μL) + DMPO (20 μL, 0.1 M). After incubating the three groups of samples at room temperature for 2 min, they were analyzed by ESR, and ESR spectral data were obtained.

PH titration was performed on the functional groups of p-BEs. PH and p-BEs (60 mg) were dispersed in 20 mL trichloromethane. The mixture was stirred for 24 h at 60 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere. The precipitate was then filtered, washed, and PH molecules were detected by UV–Vis. The supernatant was eluted until no PH molecules were present. The obtained precipitate was vacuum-dried for 12 h, named p-BEs (PH).

All calculations were performed using Gaussian 16. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted using the B3LYP functional combined with Grimme's D3BJ version dispersion correction. Geometry optimization utilized the def2-SVP basis set. Full optimization of the geometry without any structural restrictions was performed. The final energy of the fully optimized structure was calculated using a larger def2-TZVP basis set. Molecular orbital diagrams and electrostatic potential surfaces were extracted using the Multiwfn 3.8 program, and visualization was conducted using the VMD software. Configuration and conformation of p-BEs were geometrically optimized, simulating the most stable structure and Cartesian coordinates (A). Subsequently, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of p-BEs were computed.

Using HAC-NaAc buffer solution as the buffering system, different concentrations of H2O2 solution (100 μL), TMB (40 μL, 5 mM), and p-BEs (40 μL) were taken, totaling 200 μL. After reacting for 2 min, and the absorbance was measured at 652 nm. PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4, 0.01 M) was taken, and glucose solution (100 μL) and glucose oxidase (10 μL, 2.5 UN mL−1) were added. The reaction mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. After the reaction was complete, HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4, 0.2 M), TMB (40 μL, 5 mM), and p-BEs (40 μL) were added. After reacting at room temperature for 2 min, it was placed in MD, and the absorbance intensity at 652 nm was tested. For samples with low throughput, UV–Vis was used for detection.

Fish serum was used as the test sample to evaluate the actual effect of p-BEs and the accuracy of the cascade catalytic platform. Fish serum was obtained, collected in a sterile centrifuge tube, placed in a sterile centrifuge tube at 4 °C, and centrifuged at 4 °C for 10 min after blood layering. The supernatant was collected. Then, the processed blood was filtered through a 0.22 μm organic phase microporous filter to obtain 10 μL of serum. PBS buffer solution (pH = 7.4, 0.01 M) was taken, and fish serum (1 μL) and glucose oxidase (10 μL, 2.5 UN mL−1) were added, and the volume was made up to 110 μL. The reaction mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. After the reaction was complete, HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH = 4, 0.2 M), TMB (40 μL, 5 mM), and p-BEs (40 μL) were added. After reacting at room temperature for 2 min, it was placed in MD, and the absorbance intensity at 652 nm was tested. For samples with low throughput, UV–Vis was used for detection. The RGB program of the smartphone APP was used to detect the reaction samples, and the value of R/ (G + B) was determined. The glucose content in fish serum was calculated through the standard curve. The collected serum (1 μL) was dropped into a homemade drip plate and compared with the homemade standard color card for semi-quantitative analysis.

Various biomimetic materials with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) activity have been reported. However, most exhibit certain drawbacks such as environmental pollution in raw materials and complex synthesis routes. Vitamin B12, also known as cobalamin, containing cobalt, is a compound with essential physiological functions. It is the only vitamin that contains the metal element cobalt and, within acceptable doses, does not harm the body. We designed and synthesized a biomimetic enzyme by combining glutamic acid and vitamin B12, discovering its HRP-like functionality. Inspired by this, we selected L-glutamic acid and CoCl2 as raw materials. Glutamic acid, commonly used as a fish feed additive, is biologically safe even if not completely reacted, and it contains abundant functional groups such as -COOH and -NH2. Additionally, we selected amino acids with a higher content of -COOH, reasonable spatial hindrance, fewer -OH groups, and an α-amine -NH2 group near the -COOH group based on reported literature data, enhancing the catalytic activity of peroxidase (POD) [28]. Furthermore, we doped different metal elements as shown in Fig. S1A, including MnCl2·4H2O, Mg (NO3)2·6H2O, KCl, CaCl2, ZnSO4·7H2O, NaCl, and observed that Co doping exhibited stronger POD enzyme activity. This result aligns with our speculation that the biomimetic enzyme synthesized from glutamic acid and vitamin B12 has similar activity. The designed and synthesized p-BEs is a biomimetic enzyme composed of L-glutamic acid and CoCl2·6H2O. It is convenient to synthesize, and the raw materials are inexpensive and readily available. It is an excellent material choice. Even if applied directly to aquatic environments in the later stages, controlling the amount of Co within an acceptable range will not cause toxic effects on the human body.

Building upon this, we further optimized the ratio of L-Glutamic acid and CoCl2·6H2O, reaction temperature, and other factors to achieve the optimal reaction conditions for p-BEs enzyme activity. We believe that Co2+ helps avoid excessive consumption of ·OH during the formation of primary aggregates [29]. The Cl element regulates the electron cloud on the surface of n-BEs, providing potential functional groups for p-BEs, promoting substrate binding and catalysis. In this study, L-Glutamic acid and CoCl2·6H2O were used as carbon source and modifier, and the hydrothermal reaction temperature and time were identified as crucial factors influencing the catalytic performance of p-BEs. As shown in Fig. S1B, with the increase of temperature in the reaction system, the reaction rate of p-BEs catalyzing H2O2 and TMB accelerated. However, when the temperature gradually increased to 200 °C, the catalytic ability of p-BEs reached its peak. Continuing the investigation into the influence of reaction time on the catalytic performance of p-BEs, Fig. S1C indicates that increasing the hydrothermal time by 10 h or further increasing the reaction temperature does not effectively enhance the catalytic ability of p-BEs.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to study the morphology and structure of p-BEs. As shown in Fig. 1B, p-BEs exhibit an elliptical structure. Additionally, HRTEM images reveal clear lattice fringes with a spacing of approximately 0.204 nm. Moreover, the material shows good dispersion in water, providing a necessary foundation for the application of p-BEs in the field of biological analysis.

Functional groups of p-BEs were characterized using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). From Fig. 1C, distinct absorption peaks at 3440 cm−1 (composed of -OH stretching vibration), 1630 cm−1 (composed of CO, stretching vibration), and 1420 cm−1 (composed of CO stretching vibration) are observed in p-BEs. The presence of these functional groups, particularly carboxyl groups, indicates good water dispersibility. Additionally, a prominent absorption peak at 667 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of p-BEs is attributed to the stretching vibration of CCl, confirming the successful doping of CoCl2·6H2O into p-BEs [29–33].

Further, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to characterize the crystal structure of p-BEs. As shown in Fig. 1D, p-BEs form an ordered crystal structure. The diffraction peaks of p-BEs correspond well with the standard cards for C5H7NO3 (PDF#25-1781) and CoCl2(H2O)2 (PDF#72-2120) at 2θ values of 15.97427, 18.33772, 20.25474, 20.77996, 21.98794, and 26.84615. This confirms the successful doping of Co and Cl into p-BEs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to further study the composition and structure of p-BEs. According to Fig. S2A, p-BEs contain Co, O, C, and Cl elements. In high-resolution XPS spectra, the C1s peak at around 284.9 eV can be resolved into four components at 284.3 eV, 284.9 eV, 285.8 eV, and 288.4 eV (Fig. S2B), corresponding to CC, CO, CN, and CO, respectively [34]. The O1s peak at approximately 532.9 eV in high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. S2C) is composed of three components at 531.2 eV (CO), 531.8 eV (CoO), and 532.6 eV (C=O) [35]. The high-resolution Cl2p XPS spectrum (Fig. S2D) around 199.6 eV can be decomposed into two peaks corresponding to two Cl states at 198.2 eV and 199.8 eV. Additionally, the Co2p XPS peak at 781.6 eV (Fig. S2E) and the Co2p satellite peak at 796.3 eV are attributed to Co(2p3/2) and Co(2p1/2), respectively, confirming the successful doping of Co and Cl into p-BEs [36–38].

Based on preliminary experiments with peroxidase (POD) enzymes, we discovered that p-BEs exhibit catalytic activity similar to natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP). Using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as the chromogenic substrate, we employed p-BEs as the catalyst to convert H2O2 into ·OH, which further attacked TMB, oxidizing it into ox-TMB. This allowed us to evaluate the catalytic ability of p-BEs. As shown in Fig. 2A, the addition of p-BEs or n-BEs to the system with TMB and H2O2 resulted in color development (a, b), while the absence of p-BEs or H2O2 did not induce color change (c, d). Moreover, p-BEs doped with CoCl2·6H2O exhibited a deeper color change compared to non-doped n-BEs. The experiments confirmed that both p-BEs and n-BEs can catalyze the generation of ·OH from H2O2, with CoCl2·6H2O-doped p-BEs showing significantly better catalytic effects than non-doped n-BEs. Additionally, p-BEs did not directly oxidize TMB to induce color change, indicating that p-BEs possess peroxidase (POD) enzyme activity similar to HRP.

Further experiments using 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as chromogenic substrates yielded similar results. In the system containing p-BEs, ABTS, and H2O2 (Fig. 2B), p-BEs catalyzed the oxidation of ABTS to ox-ABTS, resulting in a characteristic absorption peak at 420 nm and a dark green color. Similarly, OPD was oxidized to ox-OPD, resulting in a yellow color with a characteristic peak at 451 nm. Due to the highest molar absorption coefficient (ε) of TMB among these substrates, we selected TMB as the substrate for this experiment.

Factors affecting the catalytic process of p-BEs, depend on the system's pH, biomimetic enzyme concentration, temperature, and incubation time. Initially, different pH environments (pH = 3–6) were established using acetic acid‑sodium acetate buffer to investigate the influence of pH on p-BEs catalytic activity (Fig. 2C). The results revealed that p-BEs exhibited optimal catalytic performance at pH = 4, with a sharp decrease in catalytic activity at higher pH values. This decline at pH = 5.5 may be attributed to the decomposition of H2O2 when the system's pH exceeds 5, reducing the production of ·OH during the reaction [39]. Moreover, the pH stability of p-BEs closely resembled that of HRP, with both enzymes exhibiting optimal catalytic activity in a similar pH range. Subsequently, the effect of environmental temperatures (5–65 °C) on p-BEs catalytic ability was investigated (Fig. 2D). The results showed that p-BEs maintained good catalytic activity at 5–50 °C. In contrast, HRP exhibited optimal catalytic activity only within the 30–40 °C. Therefore, p-BEs demonstrated a broader applicable temperature range, overcoming the limitations of temperature on HRP's catalytic conditions. Additionally, with the gradual increase in p-BEs concentration, the initial reaction rate of the system increased (Fig. 2E). However, when the p-BEs concentration exceeded 1.6 mg mL−1, the growth rate gradually slowed down. Considering material utilization and catalytic effectiveness, a p-BEs concentration of 1.6 mg mL−1 was chosen. Furthermore, Fig. 2F described the changes in absorbance of three reaction systems over time, indicating that the p-BEs + TMB + H2O2 system completed the reaction within 2 min, demonstrating strong catalytic capability in detecting H2O2 within a short timeframe.

Steady-state kinetics were employed to further describe the catalytic performance of p-BEs. The relationship between H2O2 and substrate concentration was shown in Fig. 3A. The initial reaction rate of the substrate with TMB as the substrate was shown in Fig. 3B. Calculations revealed that the relationship between substrate concentration and initial reaction rate was obtained by Michaelis-Menten equation. The Michaelis constants and maximum reaction rates, parameters describing the affinity of the biomimetic enzyme for the substrate and its maximum catalytic rate, were obtained using the Lineweaver-Burk equation (Fig. 3C and D, Table S1). When H2O2 was the substrate, p-BEs exhibited a significantly lower Km compared to HRP, while with TMB as the substrate, the Km values were similar. Regardless of the substrate (H2O2 or TMB), p-BEs' Vmax values were higher than those of HRP. Therefore, considering catalytic capability, cost, and stability, we concluded that p-BEs can serve as a biomimetic enzyme material replacing the function of HRP. Moreover, comparing the kinetic parameters with other biomimetic enzymes revealed that p-BEs, with H2O2 as the substrate, have an advantage in Km values. Additionally, the comparison of Vmax values across different biomimetic enzymes indicated that p-BEs possess higher catalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of free radicals in the catalytic system
ce:section.ce:para: We tested the types of catalytic processes in the system to elucidate the catalytic mechanism of p-BEs. By varying the concentration of TMB in the system with H2O2 as the substrate, we found that the resulting Lineweaver-Burk plots were nearly parallel (Fig. 4A). Similar results were obtained when using TMB as the substrate (Fig. 4B). The results showed that the catalytic process was consistent with the ping pong reaction.

Different free radical scavengers were used to study the types of free radicals produced during catalysis. Isopropanol (IPA), superoxide dismutase (SOD), and histidine (His) were used to eliminate ·OH, O2 ·-, ·OH, and 1O2, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4C, the catalytic activity of systems containing IPA and HIS was significantly reduced, while the SOD system exhibited catalytic activity similar to the blank group. With an increase in the content of HIS and IPA, the peak at 652 nm in the UV–visible spectrum gradually decreased until it disappeared (Fig. 4D, E). Additionally, chlorine (Cl) elements played a crucial role in p-BEs peroxidase activity. According to reports, the modified Cl in p-BEs can reduce H2O2 to ·OH [40]. In this process, chlorine is transformed into active chlorine.

To further validate this, we used electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy to preliminarily confirm the formation of ·OH in p-BEs mediated catalytic process. The ESR test results supported this viewpoint. The system containing p-BEs + 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) + H2O2 exhibited four characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:2:2:1 (Fig. 4F), indicative of the presence of ·OH. However, similar phenomena were not observed in the DMPO + H2O2 and p-BEs + DMPO systems. Based on the test results, it was confirmed that p-BEs activate H2O2 to generate ·OH, further oxidizing TMB to ox-TMB.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations further illustrated the existence of active sites and the catalytic mechanism of p-BEs. Based on the elemental composition and bonding forms characterization data of p-BEs, combined with the B3LYP/6-311G basis set, the local surface structure of p-BEs was optimized (Fig. S3A). Table S2 describes the atomic Cartesian coordinates (Å). Initially, one-dimensional molecular orbital theory was applied. As shown in Fig. S3B, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of p-BEs were mainly concentrated on the -C=O and Co groups, respectively, interacting with carbon cores. The relatively low ΔE (LUMO energy (ELUMO) - HOMO energy (EHOMO)) indicated that electrons were prone to transition within the molecule. Electron affinity (A), ionization potential (I), Mulliken electronegativity (χ), electrochemical potential (μ), absolute hardness (η), and electrophilicity index (ω) were calculated, and the results are shown in Table S3. These parameters are essential for understanding the surface molecular structure and catalytic performance of p-BEs. From Fig. S3C, it can be observed that the blue negative potential region is mainly located on the -C=O and Cl of the p-BEs surface. These positions readily provide electrons for nucleophilic reactions, suggesting that these locations might be potential active sites of p-BEs. The introduction of Cl elements to the p-BEs surface may induce a partial effect on the O atoms in the carbonyl group due to the inductive and conjugative effects, thereby lowering the electrostatic potential [41]. According to the previous conclusion, ·OH is generated in the catalytic process mediated by p-BEs.

Furthermore, the role of oxygen functional groups in the catalytic reaction was investigated using a chemical titration method. Phenylhydrazine (PH) was used as a specific deactivating agent for -C=O. In the same system as p-BEs, the catalytic activity of p-BEs (PH) was studied. The results indicated that the catalytic activity of p-BEs (PH) was lower than that of p-BEs (Fig. S4). This suggests that the carbonyl group significantly influences the peroxidase-like activity of p-BEs. This conclusion is consistent with the reported finding that the -C=O on the surface of p-BEs serves as an active site [42].

According to the peroxidase activity of p-BEs, the sensitivity of p-BEs to H2O2 was evaluated under optimal reaction conditions. Different concentrations of H2O2 were catalyzed by p-BEs to oxidize TMB, resulted in a blue solution, as shown in Fig. 5A. With an increase in H2O2 concentration, the absorbance of the system gradually increased, and the color of the solution deepened. The response curve of absorbance (652 nm) to H2O2 concentration is depicted in Fig. 5B. Within the range of 0.1–80 μM H2O2 concentration, the absorbance at 652 nm exhibited a well-defined linear relationship with H2O2 concentration (R2 = 0.994). Therefore, the detection method designed based on p-BEs can accurately detect biologically active substances related to H2O2 as the substrate. Glucose can be oxidized by glucose oxidase (GOD) to generate H2O2. Hence, the combination of p-BEs, sensitive to H2O2, with GOD was designed to construct a cascade catalytic sensing platform for quantitative glucose detection. Fig. 5C shows that with an increase in glucose concentration, the absorbance of the system increased, and the color changed from colorless to blue. The response curve of absorbance (652 nm) to glucose concentration is presented in Fig. 5D. Within the range of 0.1–80 μM glucose concentration, the absorbance at 652 nm exhibited a well-defined linear relationship with glucose concentration (R2 = 0.997), where Y represents the absorbance of the system at 652 nm, and X represents the glucose concentration. The established sensing platform demonstrated a low limit of quantification (LOQ) for glucose down to 0.1 μM, exhibiting favorable linear range and detection limits.

As shown in Table 1 , compared to other methods, Tang [50] and Mu [51] obtained similar LOD values of 0.04 μM. However, Mu's method required 30 min for catalysis, which was much higher than our 12 min time, while the synthesis of the bionic enzyme CuPd@H-C3N4 by Tang's method required 5 steps, which took 43 h and 30 min. Moreover, p-BEs could be synthesized by only one step, and the raw materials were more readily available and the price was lower. The catalytic effect was better, and the detection task could be completed within 12 min. Simultaneously, qualitative and semi-quantitated analysis of samples could be achieved through colorimetric comparison using color charts. Additionally, the glucose content in samples could be rapidly detected using a smartphone RGB program, with a reaction time as low as 2 min, providing a faster alternative compared to other methods and enhancing the convenience of glucose detection technology. The low limit of detection reached 0.03 μM, demonstrating accurate recognition capability for glucose.

The selectivity of the system verified the practicability of the constructed cascade catalytic sensing platform. Common ions and compounds (Na+, K+, Trp, Lys, Gly, Cys, Arg, Leu cholesterol, Fru, Lac) in fish serum were used as interference substances at concentrations 25 times that of glucose. As is known in Fig. 5E, the interference system had a minimal impact compared to the glucose system. The long-term peroxidase activity of p-BEs was tested to assess its stability for glucose detection. The p-BEs were transferred into sterile test tubes and stored at 4 °C for an extended duration. Subsequently, the same concentration of glucose was tested with p-BEs for 10 weeks to study the change of its catalytic ability. The results, as shown in Fig. 5F, indicated that the catalytic activity of p-BEs did not vary significantly from the initial value after 10 weeks of testing. The experimental results showed that p-BEs could be stored for a long time under relatively mild conditions and had excellent stability, highlighting the advantages of p-BEs over natural enzymes in storage and transportation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in real-time detection in fish serum
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted glucose detection in fish serum through two methods, as illustrated in Fig. 6A: instrumental analysis using UV–Vis and MD, and visual detection using color charts and smartphones.

Instrumental analysis using UV–Vis or MD spectroscopy involved taking a certain amount of fish serum samples and adding an appropriate amount of reactants, including p-BEs, following the experimental protocol mentioned earlier. This allowed color reactions between glucose and p-BEs to occur. Subsequently, the colored samples were placed in color dishes or microplates, and absorbance was measured at specific wavelengths. Accurate and rapid calculation of glucose content in fish serum was possible based on the standard curve.

The smartphone detection method combined the advantages of modern technology. We developed our application for glucose detection, as shown in Fig. 6B. Fish serum samples were placed under the smartphone camera, and the application utilized image processing and algorithms to calculate the glucose content in fish serum, displaying the results on the smartphone screen.

Additionally, fish serum was dropped onto a self-made microplate containing reactants, including p-BEs. Specific color changes occurred as glucose was oxidized, enabling quick visual determination of the glucose content range. This method facilitated rapid and convenient qualitative analysis.

Various types of fish were purchased to conduct comparative studies and spike tests using different methods. The results are presented in Table 2 , indicating recovery rates of 75.83 %-94.17 % at two spiked concentrations, with RSD of 0.55 %-9.16 %. The mutual confirmation of different methods ensured the consistency of the detection results.

The above methods can be used to detect glucose in fish serum. The drop plate colorimetric detection method was a simple and rapid qualitative analysis method. The advantage of using the drop plate for semi-quantitative analysis is that it can quickly determine whether the sample contains glucose and determine the content range of glucose, while the use of UV–Vis or MD can accurately determine the content of glucose in the sample, however the disadvantage is that the price is expensive, the sample delivery time is long, cannot be detected in real time, through the smart phone RGB program to solve the problem of rapid detection. It can also determine the content of glucose in serum more accurately. Comprehensive application of the above methods can improve the accuracy and reliability of detection. At the same time, these methods also have the advantages of simple operation, fast, low cost and accurate detection, and are suitable for fish farming, aquatic scientific research and fish health monitoring.

In summary, we successfully synthesized biomimetic enzyme p-BEs with peroxidase activity. The study showed that compared to peroxidase materials such as HRP, p-BEs exhibited significant advantages in terms of stability and operational conditions. The catalytic mechanism of p-BEs surface active sites was discussed. Based on this, a semi-quantitative analysis of glucose content was achieved through colorimetric comparison using color charts and microplates; quantitated analysis of glucose content was achieved by a smartphone RGB mode, UV–Vis or MD with a low LOD of 0.03 μM. The cascade catalytic sensing platform, centered around p-BEs, demonstrated strong recognition capabilities for glucose and successfully completed the task of detecting glucose in fish serum, yielding satisfactory results. Both methods ensured the accuracy and reliability of the detection results. Furthermore, p-BEs can be used to construct a series of cascade catalytic sensing platforms based on peroxidase for the detection of biologically active molecules such as glucose, xanthine, and ascorbic acid. The findings of this study present a novel avenue for the comprehensive utilization of biomimetic enzymes in the realm of agriculture and biological detection, thereby holding significant implications for nutrient detection.
